revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:機殿神社]]
'''Kan-hatori-hatadono Shrine''' (Kan-hatori-Hatadono Shrine (神服織機殿神社)) and '''Kan-omi-hatadono Shrine''' (Kan-omi-Hatadono Shrine (神麻続機殿神社)) are Shinto shrines located in {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka, Mie|1=Matsusaka}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan.
Originally situated in the same location, these shrines are believed to have been divided into two separate shrines: Kan-hatori-hatadono Shrine in Ōgaito-chō and Kan-omi-hatadono Shrine in Iguchi-naka-chō. Both shrines are associated with the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} and are collectively referred to as the '''Ryō-hatadono''' (両機殿), or "Hatadono Shrines." Their location corresponds to the former {{ill|Hatadono Village|ja|機殿村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Village|1=Hatadono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17175873}} in Iinō District, and the name "Hatadono" persists in the name of {{ill|Matsusaka City Hatadono Elementary School|ja|松阪市立機殿小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka City Hatadono Elementary School|1=Matsusaka City Kidono Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11532375}}.
The weaving ceremony of '''御衣奉織行事''' (Miso Hōshoku Ritual), held at the Ryō-hatadono shrines, has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24895900}} by Matsusaka City.
== Overview (概要) ==
{{Contains special characters}} '''Kan-hatori-Hatadono Shrine''' (Kan-hatori-Hatadono Shrine (神服織機殿神社)) and '''Kan-omi-Hatadono Shrine''' (Kan-omi-Hatadono Shrine (神麻続機殿神社)) are both '''{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} located in '''{{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka, Mie|1=Matsusaka}}, '''Mie Prefecture ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}).'''
Originally, these two shrines were '''located at the same site''', but it is believed that they later split into two separate shrines: * '''Kan-hatori-Hatadono Shrine in {{ill|Ōgaitō-chō|ja|大垣内町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgaitō-chō|1=Ōgaitō-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q97188094}}''' * '''Kan-omi-Hatadono Shrine in {{ill|Iguchinaka-chō|ja|井口中町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iguchinaka-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''
Both shrines are '''under the jurisdiction of '''{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (also known as '''Ise Grand Shrine's Inner Shrine (内宮)''').''' Collectively, they are referred to as '''"Ryō-Kidono" (両機殿, the Twin Weaving Shrines).'''
The '''location of Ryō-Kidono was originally in the former {{ill|Iino District, Mie|ja|飯野郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iino District, Mie|1=Iino district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666741}}''' at '''{{ill|Kidono Village|ja|機殿村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidono Village|1=Hatadono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17175873}}''', and the name '''"Kidono"''' remains in the present-day '''{{ill|Matsusaka City Kidono Elementary School|ja|松阪市立機殿小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka City Kidono Elementary School|1=Matsusaka City Kidono Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11532375}}.'''
The '''"Sacred Garment Weaving Ritual" (御衣奉織行事)''' conducted at both shrines has been designated as an '''Intangible Folk Cultural Property ({{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Matsusaka|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Matsusaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24895900}}) of Matsusaka City.'''
---
{| cellpadding="4" cellspacing="1" style="float:right;margin:0 0 1em 1em;border:1px solid #000;border-right-width:2px;border-bottom-width:2px;background-color:#a0f0a0;width:250px" |- ! colspan="2" style="background:#a0f0a0;text-align:center;font-size:120%" | Common Features of Ryō-Kidono |- ! style="white-space:nowrap" | {{ill|Shakaku|ja|社格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Shakaku|qid=Q10444029}} (Shrine Status) | style="background: #fff" | Under the jurisdiction of '''{{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' |- ! style="white-space:nowrap" | '''Founding''' | style="background: #fff" | '''Traditionally dated to the 25th year of '''{{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} (reigned '''29 BC – 70 AD''') |- ! style="white-space:nowrap" | '''Main Shrine Architectural Style''' | style="background: #fff" | '''{{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} (Shinmei-style architecture)''' |- ! style="white-space:nowrap" | '''Major Rituals''' | style="background: #fff" | '''Kami-Goromo Hataori Hajime-sai''' (神御衣奉織始祭, First Sacred Garment Weaving Ritual) '''Kami-Goromo Hataori Chinsha-sai''' (神御衣奉織鎮謝祭, Thanksgiving Sacred Garment Weaving Ritual) |- |}
---
== Overview == Both Kan-hatori-Hatadono Shrine (神服織機殿神社) and Kan-omi-Hatadono Shrine (神麻続機殿神社) are located on the right bank of the lower reaches of the {{ill|Kushida River|en|Kushida River|qid=Q6447936|lt=Kushida River|1=Kushida River}}. Locally, Kan-hatori-Hatadono Shrine (神服織機殿神社) is also called '''Shimohataden''' (下機殿) or '''Shimodachi''' (下館), while Kan-omi-Hatadono Shrine (神麻続機殿神社) is also known as '''Kamihataden''' (上機殿) or '''Kamidachi''' (上館). Based on their positions during the {{ill|Kamimiso-sai|ja|神御衣祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso-sai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493037|1=Kammiso-no-Matsuri}}, the 神服織 is called the right gate and 神麻績 is called the left gate. Within the precincts of both shrines there is a building larger than the main hall, a facility for weaving (hatadono, a workspace for textile weaving), which is called '''Yahirodono''' (八尋殿).
The shrines under jurisdiction are managed by {{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|qid=Q1071160|lt=Setsumusha|1=Setsumatsusha}} together with the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, and they can be broadly divided into those that, although not named in ancient ritual records, have been important for divine ceremonies, and those that were designated as shrines from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period onward for the performance of sacred rites. Since both facilities serve as the sacred grounds where offerings of the '''Nigitae''' (和妙) — a silk cloth — and the '''Aratae''' (荒妙) — a hemp cloth — for the '''Onzo''' (御衣) are prepared, they belong to the former category. However, prior to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era, the weaving facility, '''Yahirodono''' (八尋殿), which carried out the weaving work, was primary, with the shrine itself playing a subordinate role. In addition, there is another sacred ground with a shrine, the {{ill|Mishiodono Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono Shrine|1=Mishioden Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488906}}, where offerings of '''Mishio''' (御塩) are prepared; this shrine is located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|qid=Q328067|lt=Ise City|1=Ise, Mie}} and {{ill|Futami, Mie|en|Futami, Mie|qid=Q5510468|lt=Futami Town|1=Futami, Mie}}. Mishiodono Shrine, like the two facilities, is under the jurisdiction of the Imperial Grand Shrine and similarly became primary for sacred rites from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period onward.
The work of weaving the divine clothing is called '''Hōshoku''' (奉織), and the thread used is known as '''Miito''' (御糸). In the past, weaving craftsmen were referred to as '''Ninmen''' (人面), but nowadays they are called '''Oriko''' (織子).
Both facilities are uniquely located on the left bank of the '''Haraigawa''' (祓川) — a branch of the {{ill|Kushida River|en|Kushida River|qid=Q6447936|lt=Kushida River|1=Kushida River}} — west of the {{ill|Saikū|en|Saikū|qid=Q150845|lt=Saigu|1=Saikū}} and at the western end of {{ill|Kamizaka|ja|神堺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizaka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is believed that the shrines were established in regions where the textile industry flourished. The Kushida River basin was once a thriving center of textile production; in ancient times, the {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|qid=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shuui|1=Kogo Shūi}} recorded the place name as "Asamio" (麻績), and during the Shōhei era (931–937), the {{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|qid=Q2702542|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}} mentions the place names "Asamio Sato" (麻績郷) and "Kurebe Sato" (呉部郷). Moreover, during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, cotton thread produced in the upper reaches was the main product of the {{ill|Ise Merchants|ja|伊勢Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. To the east of the two facilities, north of the Saigu in {{ill|Meiwa, Mie|en|Meiwa, Mie|qid=Q1203258|lt=Meiwa Town|1=Meiwa, Mie}} in {{ill|Taki District, Mie|en|Taki District, Mie|qid=Q1042360|lt=Taki District|1=Taki District, Mie}}, the place name "Miito" still remains. Additionally, during the Edo period, the areas of the facility, upper Miito, lower Miito, east Kurobe, and west Kurobe were collectively known as '''Oitogokyō''' (御糸五郷) and were famous as a production area for Matsusaka cotton. == History ==
* In the {{ill|Wahime-myo Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wahime-myo Seiki|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131331326}}, in the 25th year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Suinin Tennō|1=Emperor Suinin}}, when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732|lt=Wahime no Mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} temporarily enshrined {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} in Ise’s Momofune (百船, Momofune), Togai-koku, Tamahiro (玉掇, Tamahiro), and Isokuni (伊蘇国), the shrine '''Hatorinoyashiro''' (神服部社) was built, which later became '''Amio Ki-dono Shinkamishiro''' (麻績機殿神服社). When the Inner Shrine (内宮) was established at its present location, a ki-dono was constructed nearby, and, in accordance with divine instruction, the granddaughter of {{ill|Ame-no-Tanahatate-hime-no-kami|ja|天棚機姫神|lt=Ame-no-Tanabatahime|1=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=天棚機姫神|lt=Ame-no-Tanabatahime|1=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=天棚機姫神|4=de|5=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|6=fr|7=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|8=zh|9=天棚機姫神|10=nl|11=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|4=de|5=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|6=fr|7=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|8=zh|9=天棚機姫神|10=nl|11=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ame-no-Tanabatahime|1=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=天棚機姫神|lt=Ame-no-Tanabatahime|1=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|2=ja|3=天棚機姫神|4=de|5=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|6=fr|7=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|8=zh|9=天棚機姫神|10=nl|11=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|4=de|5=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|6=fr|7=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|8=zh|9=天棚機姫神|10=nl|11=Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanahatate-hime-no-kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|qq=}}—'''Yachidjihime no Mikoto''' (八千々姫命)—was commissioned to weave the Nigatae (和妙).
* In the following year, the 26th year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Suinin Tennō|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732|lt=Wahime no Mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} built a weaving workshop at '''Iino Takaoka Palace''' (飯野高丘宮) and had the garments of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} woven. There, a shrine was erected and named '''Hattori-yashiro''' (服織社). In the village of '''Amio Sato''' (麻績郷), where the Amio clan resided, a coarse garment (荒衣) was likewise woven.
* In the 15th year of Hakuchi (白雉15th year , 664 AD), Iino District was separated from Taki District of Ise Province – according to the inscription on the monument of “Ise Ki-ori no Sato.”
* In the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Tenji Tennō|1=Emperor Tenji}} ({{ill|668|en|668|qid=Q30116|lt=668 AD|1=668}}), on August 3, both facilities were lost to a fire. Consequently, that year’s September work for the {{ill|Kamimiso-sai|ja|神御衣祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso-sai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493037|1=Kammiso-no-Matsuri}} was carried out in temporary structures, and later the two facilities were rebuilt separately, 30 jō apart.
* However, it is generally considered that the {{ill|Wahime-myo Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wahime-myo Seiki|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131331326}} is a tradition or legend recorded during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} by either the {{ill|Arakida clan|ja|荒木田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Togai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17217919}}, and is not regarded as historical fact. A similar account is found in the {{ill|Ise Nisho Taijingū Shinmei Hisho|ja|伊勢二所太神宮神名祕書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Taijingū Shinmei Hisho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shinmei Hisho), which—like the Wahime-myo Seiki—was recorded by Togai Yukitada in the 8th year of {{ill|Kōan (Kamakura period)|en|Kōan (Kamakura period)|qid=Q389180|lt=Kōan|1=Kōan (Kamakura period)}} ({{ill|1285|en|1285|qid=Q5546|lt=1285 AD|1=1285}}) during the Kamakura period. Although the appearance of such accounts in multiple sources might suggest some basis in fact, they are generally considered highly unlikely to be historical; nevertheless, because the record of the fire in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Tenji Tennō|1=Emperor Tenji}} appears in later works such as the {{ill|Kokki|en|Kokki|qid=Q826177|lt=Kokki|1=Kokki}}, that particular event is thought to be more historically credible.
* In sources deemed highly reliable – namely, the {{ill|Jingi-rei|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingi-rei|qq=}}, {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, and {{ill|Kōtaijingu Gishikichō|ja|皇太神宮儀式帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtaijingu Gishikichō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延暦儀式帳|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延暦儀式帳|1=Enryaku Gishikichō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109596938}} – the {{ill|Kamimiso-sai|ja|神御衣祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso-sai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493037|1=Kammiso-no-Matsuri}} is recorded, and it is certain that the weaving of the divine clothing (御衣奉織) was performed by at least the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. The Nigatae (和妙) were woven by '''Hattori''' (服部, はとりべ), while the coarse cloth (荒妙) was woven by '''Amio-bu''' (麻績部, also known as the Amio clan); each group was allocated 22 sealing doors (封戸22). The families serving the Grand Shrine were known as the Kamihattori (神服部)—descendants of {{ill|Hikozanō|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozanō|1=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487910}}—and the Kamiamio-bu (神麻績部)—descendants of {{ill|Chōhakuhakami|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōhakuhakami|1=Nagashirahano no Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11653519}}. The Hattori acquired a silk thread known as '''Akabiki no Ito''' (赤引の糸) from {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|qid=Q768158|lt=Mikawa Province|1=Mikawa Province}}, while the Amio-bu maintained a group tasked with procuring earthenware (土師器) and, having expanded into regions such as {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}} and other parts of the {{ill|Eastern Provinces|ja|東国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Provinces|1=Tōgoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10343838}}, are believed to have transmitted both weaving techniques and the {{ill|Tenpaku Shinkō|ja|天白信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Shinkō|1=Tenpaku faith|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3616095}} (please note: do not change the link for 天白神) along with their religious practices.
* In the 3rd year of {{ill|Jōryaku|en|Jōryaku|qid=Q279743|lt=Shōryaku|1=Jōryaku}} of {{ill|Emperor Shirakawa|en|Emperor Shirakawa|qid=Q60949|lt=Shirakawa Tennō|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} ({{ill|1079|en|1079|qid=Q19445|lt=1079 AD|1=1079}}), the upper facility, {{ill|Kamiamatsugi Ki-dono|ja|神麻続機殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiamatsugi Ki-dono|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was relocated to its present site.
* During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Kitabatake clan|en|Kitabatake clan|qid=Q11403853|lt=Kitabatake family|1=Kitabatake clan}}, disregarding the wishes of the {{ill|Muromachi Shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}}, usurped the shrine’s {{ill|Shinryō|ja|神領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=Jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11458058}} and expanded their influence. This area, adjacent to the Kitabatake territories, was brought under their control early on, resulting in the disappearance of both the Hattori and Amio-bu. Consequently, the two facilities came to be maintained only as modest shrines by the local populace, and the rituals and ceremonies of the Hōshoku were discontinued. Although the precise timing is uncertain, records indicate that the {{ill|Kamimiso-sai|ja|神御衣祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso-sai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493037|1=Kammiso-no-Matsuri}} was suspended for the last time in the 3rd year of {{ill|Hōtoku|en|Hōtoku|qid=Q1143606|lt=Hōtoku|1=Hōtoku}} ({{ill|1451|en|1451|qid=Q6455|lt=1451 AD|1=1451}}). The “Onzo Festival” (「{{ill|Mikka|ja|三ヶ日#祭事|lt=Onzo Festival|lt_ja=おんぞ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikka|1=Three Days|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11354062}}」) held at the shrine in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Hamamatsu|en|Hamamatsu|qid=Q185125|lt=Hamamatsu City|1=Hamamatsu}}, and {{ill|Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|en|Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu|qid=Q117077169|lt=Hamana Ward|1=Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu}}, namely at '''{{ill|Ubugi-nu Shrine|ja|初生衣神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubugi-nu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 機殿神社|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 機殿神社|1=Hatadono Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11544511}}''', is said to derive from the practice of the displaced Hattori providing the Nigatae to the Inner Shrine from {{ill|1155|en|1155|qid=Q19650|lt=1155 AD|1=1155}} onward.
* In the 2nd year of {{ill|Kōryaku|en|Kōryaku|qid=Q252777|lt=Kōryaku|1=Kōryaku}} (1361–1362) and the 5th year of {{ill|Jōji|en|Jōji|qid=Q1146153|lt=Teiji|1=Jōji}} (1366), in the village of Ryūda-gyō Fukumura (流田郷服村) where the upper facility was located,
* Through the schemes of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and his second son, {{ill|Oda Nobukatsu|en|Oda Nobukatsu|qid=Q3044815|lt=Oda Nobuo|1=Oda Nobukatsu}}, the Kitabatake family effectively perished after {{ill|Kitabatake Tomonori|en|Kitabatake Tomonori|qid=Q10903777|lt=Kitabatake Tomonori|1=Kitabatake Tomonori}}. From the time when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} unified Japan and restored public order, the number of pilgrims to the Grand Shrine began to increase. Weavers from regions such as {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} formed guilds and started to make pilgrimages to the two facilities. Owing to its proximity to the {{ill|Ise Sangu Kaidō|ja|伊勢参宮街道|lt=Pilgrimage Road|lt_ja=参宮街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sangu Kaidō|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11378967}}, the upper facility received more visitors than the lower one. Moreover, since these facilities were not directly administered by the Grand Shrine, from this period onward both began constructing shrine buildings and other structures that were disproportionate to their official status.
* In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, during the peaceful times of {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|qid=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 12 ({{ill|1699|en|1699|qid=Q7710|lt=1699 AD|1=1699}}), the {{ill|Kamimiso-sai|ja|神御衣祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso-sai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493037|1=Kammiso-no-Matsuri}} was revived and thread was offered. However, it was not until the 7th year of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period ({{ill|1874|en|1874|qid=Q7761|lt=1874 AD|1=1874}}) that the shrine’s priests resumed participation, and the practice of Hōshoku was reinstated in May of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|qid=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} period ({{ill|1914|en|1914|qid=Q2083|lt=1914 AD|1=1914}}).
* In the 3rd year of {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|qid=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō|1=Kyōhō}} ({{ill|1718|en|1718|qid=Q6899|lt=1718 AD|1=1718}}), the two facilities were repaired through a donation from the local lord, {{ill|Tsu Domain Lord|ja|津藩#歴代藩主|lt=Tsu Domain Lord|lt_ja=津藩主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsu Domain Lord|1=Tsu Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1060551}} {{ill|Fujido Takatoshi|ja|藤堂高敏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujido Takatoshi|1=Tōdō Takatoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11623748}}.
* Around the Bunka ({{ill|Bunka|en|Bunka|qid=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}}) and Bunsei ({{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|qid=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei|1=Bunsei}}) eras, the lower shrine, {{ill|Kamifukuori Ki-dono Shrine|ja|神服織機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamifukuori Ki-dono Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 機殿神社|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 機殿神社|1=Hatadono Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11544511}}, came to be known as '''Fukuta Jingū''' (服太神宮), while the upper facility became known as '''Amio Taijingū''' (麻績太神宮). The state of both facilities at that time is described in detail in the work “Taijingū Ryōgoki-dono Tsūzoku Ryakkki” (太神宮両御機殿通俗畧記) published during the {{ill|Tenpō period|ja|天保年間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (see {{ill|External Link|ja|External links|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/External Link|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). In the “Ise Sangu Meisho Zukai” (伊勢参宮名所図解) of the 9th year of {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|qid=Q1195131|lt=Kansei|1=Kansei}}, it is recorded that shrine buildings of {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|qid=Q261637|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman}} and {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日神|1=Kasuga-no-Kami|2=ja|3=春日神|4=fr|5=Kasuga no Kami|6=zh|7=春日權現|8=wuu|9=春日權現|lt=Kasuga Shrine|lt_ja=春日社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami|qq=}} were arranged on the left and right, forming a tri-shrine system. In the early Meiji work {{ill|Shin Sanko Shrines Pilgrimage Record|ja|神三郡神社参拝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Sanko Shrines Pilgrimage Record|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the left and right shrine buildings were referred to as '''Tōzai Hōden''' (東西宝殿). From the late Edo period to the early Meiji period, not only the two facilities but also other shrines not directly administered by the Grand Shrine – such as {{ill|Takihara Palace|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara Palace|1=Takihara-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566292}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|en|Izawa-no-miya|qid=Q10885171|lt=Izazu Shrine|1=Izawa-no-miya}}, and {{ill|Mishiodono Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono Shrine|1=Mishioden Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488906}} – constructed disproportionate shrine buildings.
* In the 31st year of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era ({{ill|1898|en|1898|qid=Q7848|lt=1898 AD|1=1898}}), the two facilities underwent their first reconstruction after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, being built with a keta row of 1 jō 6 shaku (approximately 4.85 m) and a beam row of 9 shaku 8 sun (approximately 2.97 m). This greatly exceeded the dimensions prescribed in the 6th year of Meiji for a first-class {{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|qid=Q1071160|lt=Setsusha|1=Setsumatsusha}} (9 shaku for the keta row and 7 shaku for the beam row), rendering them disproportionately large for their status. Even in the reconstruction of the 9th year of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} ({{ill|1934|en|1934|qid=Q18714|lt=1934 AD|1=1934}}), when their scale was reduced to that recorded in the 4th year of {{ill|Jōkyō|en|Jōkyō|qid=Q1146221|lt=Jōkyō|1=Jōkyō}} ({{ill|1687|en|1687|qid=Q7668|lt=1687 AD|1=1687}}), they still remained excessively large for a shrine under such jurisdiction.
== Divine Garment Weaving Ritual == Every year in early May and early October, at the Yahirodono of both facilities, the divine garments to be offered at the {{ill|Kōtaijingu Main Shrine|ja|皇太神宮正宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtaijingu Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the separate shrine {{ill|Arasaimiya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arasaimiya|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2859485}} during the {{ill|Kamimiso-sai|ja|神御衣祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso-sai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135493037|1=Kammiso-no-Matsuri}} are woven. This event, locally known as “'''Onzo-san'''”, was discontinued during the Sengoku period, with only a formal ritual conducted by the [[:en:Kannushi|Shinshoku]] of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, while Nigatae woven in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}} and Aratae woven in Tsukigase, Nara City (奈良市月ヶ瀬) were offered at the divine garment festival.
In May of Taishō 3, the weaving at the facilities was revived by craftsmen from {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}’s {{ill|Kisogawa, Aichi|en|Kisogawa, Aichi|qid=Q6416838|lt=Kisogawa Town|1=Kisogawa, Aichi}} (currently {{ill|Ichinomiya, Aichi|en|Ichinomiya, Aichi|qid=Q270810|lt=Ichinomiya City|1=Ichinomiya, Aichi}}’s Kisogawa Town), but the local residents found the result unsatisfactory and resolved to master and transmit the textile techniques. Since {{ill|1967|en|1967|qid=Q2648|lt=1967|1=1967}} ({{ill|1967|en|1967|qid=Q2648|lt=Showa 42|1=1967}}), the Nigatae have been woven by women inheriting the tradition from their grandfathers, whereas the Aratae continue to be woven by men. On September 27, {{ill|1975|en|1975|qid=Q2479|lt=1975|1=1975}} ({{ill|1975|en|1975|qid=Q2479|lt=Showa 50|1=1975}}), Matsusaka City designated the weaving of the Nigatae and Aratae as the “'''Divine Garment Weaving Ritual'''” and recognized it as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property.
However, the weaving carried out by local residents at both facilities accounts for only one piece each out of the 36 pieces of Nigatae required for the divine garment festival (24 pieces for the Main Shrine and 12 pieces for {{ill|Arasaimiya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arasaimiya|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2859485}}) and 120 pieces of Aratae (80 pieces for the Main Shrine and 40 pieces for {{ill|Arasaimiya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arasaimiya|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2859485}}). The remaining Nigatae and Aratae, along with other necessary items such as '''kubidama''' (くびたま) and '''tedama''' (てだま), are entrusted to specialized vendors in Kisogawa Town and Tsukigase, Nara City.
== Festivals == At both shrine compounds, two annual ceremonies are performed: the '''Kanmiso Hōshoku-hajime-sai''' (神御衣奉織始祭 – "Commencement Ritual of the Divine Garment Weaving") and the '''Kanmiso Hōshoku-chinsha-sai''' (神御衣奉織鎮謝祭 – "Thanksgiving Ritual on Completion of the Divine Garment Weaving").
=== Kanmiso Hōshoku-hajime-sai === This ceremony is held to pray that the weaving may proceed in purity and beauty. It is celebrated on 1 May and 1 October each year: 08:00 at the Lower Weaving Hall (''Shimokidono'') and 09:00 at the Upper Weaving Hall (''Kamikidono''). Local residents engaged in the weaving first undergo {{ill|Lustration (Shinto)|de|Misogi (Shintō)|ja|潔斎|zh|潔斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lustration (Shinto)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the shrine precincts’ purification lodge (''Saikan''). After donning white robes (''hakama'' and ''haori''), they sprinkle salt for further purification and clean the interior of the ''Yahiro-den'' weaving hall. The looms and tools are set up, the sacred threads installed, and after the priests’ worship the weaving begins.
While the volunteer weavers return home in the evenings, priests dispatched from the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} enter the ''Saikan'' the day before, remain in a state of ritual purity, and stay until the completion ceremony. From the second day onward the weavers report each morning, cleanse themselves, put on white robes, and work in the ''Yahiro-den'' until the task is finished.
=== Kanmiso Hōshoku-chinsha-sai === Held on 13 May and 13 October at 08:00, this rite offers thanks for the safe completion of the weaving and dedicates a silk and a hemp robe. After the rite, the two robes are sealed in lacquered chests and borne roughly 20 km to the Inner Shrine (''Naikū''). Since the 1950s they have been transported by car; previously the procession left around 21:00, walked the old pilgrimage road through the night, crossed the {{ill|Miya River (Mie)|en|Miya River (Mie)|qid=Q6884164|lt=Miya River (Mie)|1=Miya River (Mie)}} by boat, rested briefly at the {{ill|Outer Shrine (Ise)|de|Gekū|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine (Ise)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Toyōke Daijingū|qid=Q11633343}}, and arrived at the Inner Shrine about 05:00 on the 14th. Two members of the {{ill|Ise Shrine guard|de|Eji (Ise-jingū)|ja|神宮衛士|zh|神宮衛士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shrine guard|1=Jingū Guards|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11589497}} preceded the procession, accompanied by a deputy senior priest (''Gon-negī'') and a palace steward (''Miyatsukasa'').
== Kan-hatori Hatadono Shrine == {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kan-hatori Hatadono Jinja''' (神服織機殿神社) | image = File:Kan-hatori-hatadono-jinja 07.JPG | image_caption = Honden (left) and Yahiro-den (right) | location = {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka, Mie|1=Matsusaka}}, Ōgaito-chō, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.579111|N|136.606861|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = "Guardian deity of the Kan-hatori Weaving Hall"<br />(traditionally {{ill|Amenomihoko|de|Amenomihoko|ja|天御桙命|zh|天御桙命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenomihoko|1=amenomihokonomikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119380365}} and {{ill|Amenoyachijihime|de|Ama-no-yachiji-hime|ja|天八千々姫|zh|天八千千姬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenoyachijihime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_caption = Location within Mie Prefecture | prefecture= {{ill|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}} }} [[File:Kan-hatori-hatadono-jinja 01.JPG|thumb|200px|General view of the shrine precincts]] [[File:Kan-hatori-hatadono-jinja_08.JPG|left|thumb|160px|Weaving of the silk robe (''wata-tamaki'')]]
=== Weaving of the Silk Robe (''Wata-tamaki'') === The silk robe (''nagusa'') is woven in the ''Yahiro-den'' within the precincts. Female weavers report at 08:00 each morning. They wear white robes but spread a black cloth over their knees to better see the fine silk threads. Because rough fingertips catch on the filaments, great care is taken to keep the fingers smooth.
Even today silk comes from the {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|qid=Q768158|lt=Mikawa Province|1=Mikawa Province}} region of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. Four single filaments make one thread; thirty-six threads form one bundle (''azari''); and sixty-seven bundles provide the warp, yielding a fabric 1 shaku 5 sun (≈ 45 cm) wide. Thus 4 × 36 × 67 = 9 648 filaments are required. Joining the finite-length filaments and setting the warp on the loom alone takes about three days, so pre-joined warps prepared by specialists are now used to shorten the weaving period. The weft, soaked in water beforehand, is wound on bobbins from seven to nine filaments.
A bolt of 4 jō (≈ 12.1 m) usually takes four to five days to weave and then several more to dry. The finished silk is boxed and placed on a shelf until the Thanksgiving ceremony.
{{clear}}
== Kan-omi Hatadono Shrine == {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kan-omi Hatadono Jinja''' (神麻続機殿神社) | image = [[File:Kan-omi-hatadono-jinja_04.JPG|250px]] | image_caption = Honden | location = Matsusaka, Mie, Iguchi-naka-chō | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|45.9|N|136|36|4.2|E|type:landmark}} | deity = "Guardian deity of the Kan-omi Weaving Hall"<br />(traditionally {{ill|Amenoyasakahiko|de|Ama-no-yasakahiko|ja|天八坂彦命|zh|天八坂彥命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenoyasakahiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_caption = Location within Mie Prefecture }} [[File:Kan-omi-hatadono-jinja 01.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Shrine precincts]]
=== Weaving of the Hemp Robe (''Arata-tamaki'') === [[File:Kan-omi-hatadono-jinja_14.JPG|left|thumb|200px|Hemp weaving in progress]] The hemp robe (''arata'') is woven in the ''Yahiro-den'' on this precinct. Male weavers report at 08:00 each morning, wear white robes, and likewise guard their fingertips. After setting the warp and winding the weft bobbins, the threads are soaked.
Formerly hemp came from the nearby {{ill|Miozono (Matsusaka)|de|Miozono|ja|御麻生薗|zh|御麻生薗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miozono (Matsusaka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, whose name reflects the ancient hemp fields of the Grand Shrine. Today hemp is sourced from the Tsukigase district of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}}.
Using 910 warp threads, a bolt 1 shaku (≈ 30 cm) wide and 4 jō 1 sun (≈ 12.4 m) long is woven by a four-man team: one operates the loom, one alternates the ''shinshi'' stretchers, and two flank the loom ready to repair broken threads.
Because the tensile strength of hemp varies sharply with humidity, progress depends greatly on the weather. Normally the work finishes in five to six days, but in delays the weavers may labour by candle-light into the night and need up to ten days.
After several days’ drying the hemp cloth is placed in the hall until the Thanksgiving ceremony.
{{clear}}
== Sub-shrines == Each precinct contains eight small subsidiary shrines enshrining guardian deities (''Omae-no-kami''); their individual kami are unknown.
== Buildings == [[File:Kan-hatori-hatadono-jinja 06.JPG|thumb|160px|Honden (left) and Yahiro-den (right) at the Lower Hall]] Both shrines follow the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style, facing south, with inward-facing forked finials (''chigi'') and six horizontal billets (''katsuogi'')—an even number like the Inner Shrine. East of each ''Honden'' stands the ''Yahiro-den'' weaving hall, larger than the main sanctuary and roofed in thatch with eight ''katsuogi'' and inward-facing ''chigi''. Unlike the ''Honden'', the hall has its own torii. Three sides (except the east) bear doors opened during weaving.
Both buildings are enclosed by separate board fences (''itagaki''); two small shrines stand at the ''Honden’s'' flanks. A purification lodge (''Saikan'') is located south of each compound for ritual seclusion.
== Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|qid=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|qid=Q900630|lt=Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|1=Yamada Line (Kintetsu)}} {{ill|Koishiro Station|en|Koishiro Station|qid=Q6426352|lt=Koshinoe Station|1=Koishiro Station}}. From the station it is about 2.5 km north to the Upper Hall and another 2 km to the Lower Hall, just south of National Route 23.
=== See also === * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} * {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka, Mie|1=Matsusaka}} * {{ill|Loom|en|Loom|qid=Q173056|lt=Loom|1=Loom}} * {{ill|Fukui Bunemon|de|Fukui Bun’emon|ja|福井文右衛門|zh|福井文右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Bunemon|1=fukui bun'emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q69634923}}
== References == {{No footnotes|date=October 2019|section=1}} * ''Kanmiso & Arata: The Divine Robes of the Grand Shrine'' (Jingū Administration Office, 2005). * ''Matsusaka City History, vol. 3: Sources, Ancient & Medieval'' (Matsusaka City, 1980). * ''Matsusaka City History, vol. 6: Cultural Properties'' (Matsusaka City, 1979). * ''Matsusaka City History, vol. 10: Folk Customs'' (Matsusaka City, 1981). * Katsu Sakurai, ''The Archetype and Development of Ise Grand Shrine'' (1991, ISBN 4-336-03296-3). * Katsu Sakurai, ''Ise Grand Shrine'' (1969). * Jingū Administration Office, ''O-Ise Mairi'' (7th rev. ed., 2006). * ''Kadokawa Gazetteer of Japan 24: Mie Prefecture'' (Kadokawa Shoten, 1983).

== References == * *Jingū no Nigitama to Aratama* (神宮の和妙と荒妙), Jingū Office, 2005. * *Matsusaka City History, Volumes 3, 6, and 10*, edited by Matsusaka City History Editorial Committee. * Sakurai Katsunosuke, *The Prototype and Development of Ise Grand Shrine*, Kokushokankōkai, 1991.

<!--「掇」ひろう、とる--><!-- A shrine template was considered, but since location details are mandatory in the template, it was not used. Please adjust this table accordingly if needed. --><!--From "Walking in Ise-Shima", page 31, though its meaning is unclear—whether it refers to a priest or a sacred casket...--><!--In "Walking in Ise-Shima", it is read as "hitomo"--><!-- It might be desirable here to include an account of Taki District and Iino District becoming a sacred district. --><!-- It may be excessively redundant to include here the account of the Kitabatake and Kuki clans’ seizure of the sacred estate. --><!-- It is desirable to provide subsequent size specifications. --><!--『神宮の和妙と荒妙』による。『伊勢志摩を歩く』によれば下機殿午前8時、上機殿午前9時--><!--『角川日本地名大辞典』では「みょうぞの」だが、そんな読みは聞いたことがないので無視--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines--><!--Hatadono Shrines-->
